ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for a 50:50 brown/white rice blend; the closest defensible parent class is cooked rice food product (FOODON_03311069).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers cooked rice generically; no specific code exists for a 50:50 brown/white blend. Used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for a 50:50 brown/white rice blend; left null to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard rice bowl serving of mixed brown and white rice (50:50 blend) is approximately 180g of cooked rice.

nutrient_source.title: Mixed Brown Rice (50% Brown, 50% White) — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. All per_100g values are taken directly from the HPB Foodo entry for this specific 50:50 brown/white rice blend. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is set to -1. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, cholesterol, vitamins, and other micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a 50:50 brown/white rice blend in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated as the arithmetic mean of brown rice (GI ~55, low-medium boundary) and white rice (GI ~64–72, high) cooked values from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The 50:50 blend GI is conservatively anchored at 55. Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low category (low: ≤55). gi_category is therefore set to 'low' in strict accordance with the University of Sydney threshold (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 26.9 × 1.80) / 100 = 26.6 per 180g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (3 mg/100g) and negligible added sugar (0.15 g/100g)
hypertension.guidance: This 50:50 brown/white rice blend is suitable for individuals with hypertension; sodium is negligible at 3 mg/100g, and pairing with potassium-rich vegetables such as spinach or broccoli will further support DASH-diet blood pressure targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low GI (55, upper boundary) but high glycemic load per serving (26.6) and low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 100–120g cooked, pair with non-starchy vegetables and a lean protein source to blunt the postprandial glucose response, and consider upgrading to 100% brown rice or red/black rice for better glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low fat (0.63 g/100g), negligible sodium (3 mg/100g), and no cholesterol data indicating a low saturated-fat staple
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This rice blend is a low-fat, low-sodium carbohydrate staple appropriate for a heart-healthy diet; portion control to 150–180g cooked is advised, and combining with soluble-fibre-rich legumes or vegetables will further support LDL cholesterol management.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low dietary fibre (1.0 g/100g) and absence of anti-inflammatory micronutrients
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: This blend does not meet the >3 g/100g whole-food fibre threshold for a positive anti-inflammatory contribution; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should pair it with omega-3-rich fish, turmeric-spiced dishes, or legumes to provide anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mixed Brown and White Rice (50:50 Blend) is a cooked rice staple prepared by combining equal proportions of brown rice and white rice before or after cooking. Brown rice retains its bran and germ layers, contributing a slightly nutty flavour, firmer texture, and marginally higher fibre and micronutrient content compared to milled white rice. The 50:50 blend offers a middle-ground texture — softer than pure brown rice yet with a subtle chewiness absent from plain white rice. Per 100g cooked, the blend provides 127 kcal, 2.72g protein, 0.63g fat, 26.9g carbohydrate, 1.0g dietary fibre, and only 3mg sodium, making it a low-sodium, low-fat carbohydrate staple. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low, upper boundary per University of Sydney classification), yielding a glycemic load of approximately 26.6 per standard 180g bowl serving. Clinically, the blend is suitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management due to its negligible sodium and fat content, but requires portion control and dietary pairing for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes given its high glycemic load per serving. The fibre content at 1.0g/100g is insufficient to confer significant anti-inflammatory benefit for rheumatoid arthritis. This blend is widely consumed in Singapore and Southeast Asia as a health-conscious alternative to plain white rice, often served alongside protein dishes, stir-fried vegetables, and soups.

confidence_reason: All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database with a reported confidence of 0.95 and review_status of reviewed. The GI is an analogous estimate anchored to University of Sydney GI Database values for brown and white rice, which is the best available approved evidence for this specific blend.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB for this entry): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are not reported in the HPB Foodo entry for this specific 50:50 brown/white rice blend as of the accessed date. Detailed fatty acid fractions and full micronutrient profiles for this specific blend are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as a named composite entry. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate (arithmetic mean of brown rice GI ~55 and white rice GI ~64 from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008); no direct GI measurement for a 50:50 blend was found in the approved GI sources. Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification thresholds (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70), GI 55 is classified as 'low'. gi_category has been corrected from 'medium' to 'low' to align with this threshold. Glycemic load per serving calculated at 180g serving: (55 × 26.9 × 1.80) / 100 = 26.6. This food is a generic staple with no cuisine-specific mandatory multilingual naming requirement; local_names fields are populated based on the food being a common staple in Singapore, thus requiring Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi names.

Updated gi_source.source_match_notes to explicitly document the University of Sydney classification threshold and the rationale for 'low' categorisation.
